cards.title: Heliotherapy — Principles & Mechanisms
cards._legacyHtml:
Definition: Therapeutic use of sunlight for healing. One of the oldest naturopathic modalities (Hippocrates, Arnold Rikli, Auguste Rollier).
Solar spectrum components:
• UV-A (315–400 nm): Tanning, Vitamin D precursor activation, mood elevation
• UV-B (280–315 nm): Vitamin D3 synthesis (7-dehydrocholesterol → cholecalciferol), psoriasis treatment
• Infrared (700 nm–1 mm): Heat, vasodilation, muscle relaxation, pain relief
• Visible light: Circadian rhythm regulation, serotonin production
Physiological effects: Vasodilation, bactericidal action, Vitamin D synthesis, serotonin boost, endorphin release, nitric oxide release (BP reduction), immune modulation.

cards.title: Sun Bath — Types & Technique
cards._legacyHtml:
Full body sun bath: Expose maximum body surface. Start 5 min (sensitive skin) to 15 min. Increase by 5 min every 2–3 days. Maximum 45 min.
Partial sun bath: Only affected area exposed — used for skin conditions, arthritic joints.
Best time: 7:00–9:00 AM (UV-B available, intensity moderate). Avoid 10 AM–4 PM (excessive UV).
Position: Supine. Cover eyes with wet cloth or UV-protective goggles. Head in shade or covered.
Sequence: 5 min front → turn → 5 min back. Increase gradually.
Post-sun bath: Cold water sponge or shower immediately after to prevent overheating. Rest for 15–20 min.
Environment: Open air, clean location. Avoid polluted urban areas. Best in natural settings.

cards.title: Vitamin D Protocol — Clinical
cards._legacyHtml:
Synthesis requirement: UV-B 290–315 nm. 20–30 min full body exposure (40% skin exposed) in morning sun = 10,000–20,000 IU Vitamin D3 synthesis in fair skin.
Factors reducing synthesis: Dark skin, sunscreen (SPF 8 blocks 95% UV-B), age (>70 yrs: 75% reduction), glass windows, cloud cover, winter, high latitude.
Clinical deficiency (Vit D <20 ng/ml) protocol:
• Daily morning sun 20–30 min (arms + legs exposed)
• Dietary: fatty fish, egg yolks, fortified milk, mushrooms
• Supplementation if needed: 2,000–4,000 IU/day (D3 + K2 MK-7 100 mcg)
Monitoring: 25-OH Vitamin D at baseline and 3 months.
Target: 40–60 ng/ml (naturopathic optimal). Toxicity risk above 150 ng/ml (only with supplementation, not sun exposure).

cards.title: Air Bath (Aerotherapy)
cards._legacyHtml:
Definition: Exposure of the body to fresh open air, with skin as much exposed as possible, without direct sun exposure.
Benefits: Skin hardening (cold air stimulation), improved circulation, nervous system toning, increased oxygen uptake, immune stimulation, psychological uplift.
Types:
• Cold air bath: 10–20°C. 5–15 min. For robust individuals. Stimulating.
• Warm air bath: 20–25°C. 20–30 min. For sensitive, elderly, children.
Technique: Remove clothing as much as comfortable. Walk, perform mild exercises (deep breathing, arm swings) during the bath. Begin with 5 min; increase by 5 min every 3 days.
Indications: General debility, nervous disorders, skin conditions, chronic respiratory ailments, convalescence.
Best location: Near sea, forest, mountains — negative ion-rich environments.

cards.title: Forest Bathing (Shinrin-Yoku)
cards._legacyHtml:
Origin: Japanese practice (1982). Therapeutic immersion in forest environment — not hiking but slow, mindful presence among trees.
Active agents: Phytoncides (antimicrobial terpenes: alpha-pinene, limonene, cedrol) released by trees → inhaled → immune modulation.
Evidence-based effects:
• NK cell activity ↑ 50% after 3-day forest stay (persists 30 days) — Qing Li, 2007
• Cortisol ↓ 12.4% vs city walk
• BP ↓ (systolic ~7 mmHg)
• Parasympathetic activity ↑ (HRV improvement)
• Depression and anxiety scores ↓
Protocol: 2–4 hours in forest, slow pace, engage all 5 senses, no phones. 1–2 times/week minimum.
Prescription: Ideal adjunct for hypertension, anxiety, depression, cancer recovery, chronic fatigue.

cards.title: Bright Light Therapy & SAD
cards._legacyHtml:
Indication: Seasonal Affective Disorder (SAD), non-seasonal depression, circadian rhythm disorders, shift work disorder, jet lag, insomnia.
Device: 10,000 lux light box (full-spectrum white light, UV-filtered).
Protocol: 20–30 min exposure each morning within 30–60 min of waking. Sit 30–40 cm from box. Eyes open but not staring directly at light.
Onset: Response in 3–5 days. Full effect by 2 weeks.
Evidence: Level 1A for SAD. Comparable to antidepressants for non-seasonal depression (Lam et al., Lancet 2016).
Contraindications: Bipolar disorder (risk of mania), retinal conditions (macular degeneration, diabetic retinopathy — ophthalmologist clearance needed), photosensitising medications (lithium, tetracyclines, St John's Wort).
Side effects: Headache, eyestrain, nausea (reduce session to 15 min if occurs).

cards.title: Heliotherapy — Indications & Contraindications
cards._legacyHtml:
Indications: Rickets, osteomalacia, osteoporosis, psoriasis, vitiligo, lupus vulgaris (TB of skin), acne, eczema, jaundice (neonatal — blue light preferred), anaemia, debility, depression, SAD, chronic fatigue, fibromyalgia, hypertension (indirect via NO), immune deficiency.

Contraindications:
• Active tuberculosis (systemic)
• Acute febrile conditions
• Systemic lupus erythematosus (SLE)
• Photosensitive skin disorders (porphyria, xeroderma pigmentosum)
• Photosensitising medications (tetracyclines, sulfonamides, quinolones, amiodarone, St John's Wort)
• History of melanoma or multiple skin cancers
• Cardiac failure (decompensated)

Precautions: Always cover head and eyes. Avoid between 10 AM–4 PM (peak UV). Gradual acclimatisation. Hydrate well before and after.
